top_issues.file: index.js, lib/api.js, lib/auth.js, lib/http.js, lib/multipart.js, lib/plugin.js
top_issues.issue: 182 active ESLint errors — all source files from the recent refactoring use double-quoted strings, violating the project's single-quote rule. CI-blocking.
Dead Connection ES6 class that is never imported or used. The runtime Connection is still a constructor function in index.js. Refactoring artifact causing confusion.
Module-level singleton plugin registry (const plugins = Object.create(null)). Tests are not isolated; state leaks between test runs and between tenants in serverless environments.
OAuth object is a plain untyped object throughout the codebase. No validation at call sites. Missing fields produce obscure TypeError crashes at runtime.
revokeToken hardcodes Salesforce OAuth revoke endpoint URLs instead of using the configurable loginUri/testLoginUri connection options. Breaks enterprise custom-domain deployments.
OptionHelper is an unnecessary constructor-factory wrapper around two pure functions. No state, no defaults. Callers must invoke require('./optionhelper')() which is surprising.
getUrl/putUrl/postUrl/deleteUrl repeat the same opts.oauth.instance_url + requireForwardSlash(opts.url) URL construction pattern four times. Body serialization also duplicated.
_queryHandler is included in module.exports despite being a private implementation method. Unnecessarily expands the public API surface.
isObject(null) returns true because typeof null === 'object' in JavaScript. Latent correctness bug for any caller passing null.
let self = this anti-pattern used in two ES6 class constructors. Arrow functions are the modern idiomatic replacement for Node.js 22 targets.
Template literal at line 33 contains a stray single-quote character, making the expected URL value incorrect. The url assertion silently never fires due to promise error handler swallowing it.
Teardown calls client.logout() which does not exist on Connection. Would throw TypeError if the integration test ever ran with real credentials.
